expanded_description:
The Bank of Canada is the nation's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight. Founded in 1934, the Bank operates under the Bank of Canada Act and has a unique governance structure: the Governing Council (Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors) sets monetary policy, while the Board of Directors (appointed by the Minister of Finance) oversees management. Since the COVID-19 pandemic (April 2020), the Bank has operated a floor system (similar to the Federal Reserve) rather than its traditional corridor system, due to the large-scale asset purchases (QE) that created ample settlement balances in the banking system. The Bank targets the overnight rate, using the deposit rate as a floor and standing liquidity facilities as needed.

Understanding the Bank of Canada's structure is essential for analyzing how monetary policy is made in Canada, how the Bank implements its policy rate (the overnight rate target), and how it has evolved in response to the pandemic. Before COVID, the Bank used a corridor system (target ± 25bp, active open market operations). Since April 2020, with ample reserves from QE, the Bank has shifted to a floor system (deposit rate floor, passive operations). This change aligns Canada with the Federal Reserve's implementation framework. The Bank also has a strong focus on inflation targeting (2% target since 1991), making it one of the most successful inflation-targeting central banks.

In this section, we will examine the Bank of Canada's governance structure (Governing Council, Board of Directors), its core functions (monetary policy, lender of last resort, currency issuance, payment systems, fiscal agent), its monetary policy implementation framework (historical corridor system vs. current floor system), and its approach to financial stability. You will learn to understand the Bank's current floor system, compare it with the Fed's framework, and evaluate the Bank's inflation-targeting framework.

core_concept.principle:
The Bank of Canada is Canada's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight. Since the COVID-19 pandemic, the Bank operates a floor system (ample reserves) for implementing monetary policy, having shifted from its traditional corridor system.

Governance Structure:

• Governing Council: Sets monetary policy. Comprises the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors.
• Board of Directors: Oversees management (not monetary policy). Comprises the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and 12 outside directors appointed by the Minister of Finance.
• Governor: Appointed by the Board (with Cabinet approval) for a 7-year term (renewable).
• The Bank is accountable to Parliament through the Minister of Finance.

Core Functions:

1. Monetary policy: Inflation targeting (2% target since 1991). Sets overnight rate target.
2. Lender of last resort: Provides liquidity to solvent but illiquid financial institutions.
3. Currency issuance: Designs, produces, and distributes bank notes.
4. Payment system oversight: Operates and oversees large-value payment systems (Lynx, formerly LVTS).
5. Fiscal agent for the Government of Canada: Manages federal debt, auctions bonds, processes payments.
6. Foreign exchange reserves management: Manages Canada's official international reserves.

Historical Corridor System (Pre-COVID, pre-April 2020):

• Overnight rate target ± 25 basis points (e.g., target 5.00%, band 4.75% to 5.25%).
• Deposit rate (floor): interest paid on settlement balances (bottom of band).
• Bank Rate (ceiling): interest charged on overnight loans to financial institutions (top of band).
• Active open market operations (SPRAs, LLAs) to keep overnight rate near target.
• Scarce reserves (reserves just sufficient to meet demand).

Current Floor System (Post-COVID, since April 2020):

• Deposit rate (interest on settlement balances) acts as a floor for the overnight rate.
• Banks will not lend reserves below the deposit rate (can earn risk-free return).
• The overnight rate target is set at the deposit rate (or slightly above).
• Ample reserves from QE (quantitative easing) mean the Bank no longer needs to fine-tune daily.
• The Bank Rate (ceiling) is set 25bp above the deposit rate (rarely used).
• Open market operations are passive; the Bank lets reserves fluctuate.

Why the Shift?

• During COVID-19 (March-April 2020), the Bank engaged in large-scale asset purchases (QE), injecting massive reserves into the banking system.
• Reserves became ample (from \$10-20 billion to over \$300 billion).
• With ample reserves, the corridor system (which required scarce reserves) became unworkable.
• The Bank shifted to a floor system, aligning with the Federal Reserve's framework.

Comparison: Bank of Canada (Floor) vs. Federal Reserve (Floor):

FeatureBank of Canada (Current)Federal Reserve
Operating frameworkFloor systemFloor system
Primary toolDeposit rate (floor)IORB (floor for banks)
ReservesAmple (since QE)Ample (since 2008)
Open market operationsPassivePassive
CeilingBank Rate (discount window)Discount window
Non-bank floorNone (but CAD is less dollarized)ON RRP facility


Inflation Targeting (since 1991):

• Target: 2% inflation (CPI), within a range of 1-3%.
• The Bank adjusts the overnight rate to achieve the target.
• Canada was one of the first countries to adopt inflation targeting (along with New Zealand).
• The framework has been successful: inflation has averaged 2% since 1991.
core_concept.explanation:
Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the Bank of Canada operated a traditional corridor system. The Bank kept reserves scarce (just enough to meet demand) and used daily open market operations (SPRAs and LLAs) to keep the overnight rate within a 50 basis point band around the target. This required active management.

When the pandemic hit in March 2020, the Bank launched large-scale asset purchases (QE), buying government bonds and injecting massive reserves into the banking system. Settlement balances (reserves) grew from about \$10-20 billion to over \$300 billion. With reserves now ample, the corridor system no longer worked because the Bank could not fine-tune such a large quantity. The Bank shifted to a floor system.

Under the floor system, the deposit rate (interest on settlement balances) acts as a floor. Banks will not lend reserves below the deposit rate because they can earn that rate risk-free by leaving them at the Bank. The overnight rate target is set at the deposit rate (or slightly above). The Bank Rate (ceiling) is set 25bp above the deposit rate as a backstop. With ample reserves, the overnight rate stays near the deposit rate without active open market operations. This is the same framework the Federal Reserve has used since 2008.

The shift to a floor system was a major operational change for the Bank of Canada. It aligns Canada with the US and other major central banks. The Bank still uses open market operations occasionally (e.g., for year-end liquidity management), but daily fine-tuning is no longer needed.

For Canadians, this change is mostly invisible. The Bank still sets the overnight rate target, and that rate still influences mortgage rates and other borrowing costs. But the implementation is now simpler and more reliable.

image.alt: Graph: Deposit rate floor (4.75%), overnight rate trading near floor, Bank Rate ceiling (5.25%); ample reserves supply curve flat at deposit rate.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.1: The Bank of Canada's current floor system (post-COVID). The deposit rate acts as a floor; ample reserves from QE keep the overnight rate near the floor without active open market operations.

sections.introduction:
The Bank of Canada is the nation's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight. Founded in 1934, the Bank operates under the Bank of Canada Act and has a unique governance structure: the Governing Council (Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors) sets monetary policy, while the Board of Directors (appointed by the Minister of Finance) oversees management. Since the COVID-19 pandemic (April 2020), the Bank has operated a floor system (similar to the Federal Reserve) rather than its traditional corridor system, due to the large-scale asset purchases (QE) that created ample settlement balances in the banking system. The Bank targets the overnight rate, using the deposit rate as a floor and standing liquidity facilities as needed.

Understanding the Bank of Canada's structure is essential for analyzing how monetary policy is made in Canada, how the Bank implements its policy rate (the overnight rate target), and how it has evolved in response to the pandemic. Before COVID, the Bank used a corridor system (target ± 25bp, active open market operations). Since April 2020, with ample reserves from QE, the Bank has shifted to a floor system (deposit rate floor, passive operations). This change aligns Canada with the Federal Reserve's implementation framework. The Bank also has a strong focus on inflation targeting (2% target since 1991), making it one of the most successful inflation-targeting central banks.

In this section, we will examine the Bank of Canada's governance structure (Governing Council, Board of Directors), its core functions (monetary policy, lender of last resort, currency issuance, payment systems, fiscal agent), its monetary policy implementation framework (historical corridor system vs. current floor system), and its approach to financial stability.

introduction_image.alt: Timeline: Pre-COVID (corridor system, scarce reserves, active OMOs) → COVID-19 (QE, ample reserves) → Post-COVID (floor system, passive OMOs).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 6.5.2: Evolution of the Bank of Canada's monetary policy implementation framework. Pre-COVID: corridor system with scarce reserves. Post-COVID: floor system with ample reserves.

key_concept.definition:
Bank of Canada: Canada's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight.

Governance Structure:

• Governing Council: Sets monetary policy. Comprises the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors.
• Board of Directors: Oversees management (not monetary policy). Comprises the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and 12 outside directors appointed by the Minister of Finance.
• Governor: Appointed by the Board (with Cabinet approval) for a 7-year term (renewable).

Core Functions:

1. Monetary policy: Inflation targeting (2% target since 1991). Sets overnight rate target.
2. Lender of last resort: Provides liquidity to solvent but illiquid financial institutions.
3. Currency issuance: Designs, produces, and distributes bank notes.
4. Payment system oversight: Operates and oversees large-value payment systems (Lynx, formerly LVTS).
5. Fiscal agent for the Government of Canada: Manages federal debt, auctions bonds, processes payments.
6. Foreign exchange reserves management: Manages Canada's official international reserves.

Historical Corridor System (Pre-COVID, pre-April 2020):

• Overnight rate target ± 25 basis points.
• Deposit rate (floor), Bank Rate (ceiling).
• Active open market operations (SPRAs, LLAs).
• Scarce reserves.

Current Floor System (Post-COVID, since April 2020):

• Deposit rate (interest on settlement balances) acts as a floor.
• Banks will not lend reserves below the deposit rate.
• Overnight rate target set at deposit rate (or slightly above).
• Ample reserves from QE.
• Bank Rate set 25bp above deposit rate (ceiling).
• Passive open market operations.

Why the Shift?

• COVID-19 QE injected massive reserves (from \$10-20B to over \$300B).
• Corridor system requires scarce reserves; floor system works with ample reserves.
• Aligns with Federal Reserve framework.

Inflation Targeting (since 1991):

• Target: 2% inflation (CPI), within a range of 1-3%.
• The Bank adjusts the overnight rate to achieve the target.
• Canada was one of the first countries to adopt inflation targeting.
key_concept.context: The Bank of Canada was founded in 1934 as a private institution, becoming a Crown corporation in 1938. It adopted inflation targeting in 1991, becoming a pioneer. Before COVID, the Bank used a corridor system (target ± 25bp) with scarce reserves, requiring daily open market operations. In March-April 2020, the Bank launched QE, purchasing government bonds and injecting massive reserves. By April 2020, settlement balances had grown from \$10-20 billion to over \$300 billion. With ample reserves, the corridor system became unworkable. The Bank announced a shift to a floor system, effective April 2020. This change aligns Canada with the Federal Reserve (which has used a floor system since 2008). The Bank still uses its deposit rate as the floor and the Bank Rate as a backstop ceiling.

image.alt: Two graphs side by side: Historical corridor system (scarce reserves, active OMOs) and Current floor system (ample reserves, deposit rate floor).
image.caption: Figure 6.5.3: The Bank of Canada's shift from corridor system (pre-COVID) to floor system (post-COVID). The floor system uses the deposit rate as a floor and requires no active open market operations.

classifications.type: Governance: Governing Council vs. Board of Directors
classifications.value: The Governing Council sets monetary policy; the Board of Directors oversees management and administration
characteristics: Governing Council: Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, 4 Deputy Governors (6 members total).
Governing Council meets 8 times per year to set the overnight rate target.
Board of Directors: Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, 12 outside directors appointed by Minister of Finance.
Board oversees management, approves budget, appoints auditors, but does not set monetary policy.
Governor appointed for 7-year term (renewable) by the Board with Cabinet approval.
The Bank is accountable to Parliament through the Minister of Finance (regular testimony).
classifications.behavior:
The Bank of Canada's governance structure separates monetary policy (Governing Council) from administration (Board of Directors). This is similar to many central banks. The Governing Council's small size (6 members) allows for efficient decision-making. The Governor is the chief executive officer and primary spokesperson. The Governor's 7-year term insulates him or her from political pressure.

The Board of Directors includes the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and 12 outside directors from across Canada (representing regions). The Board ensures the Bank is well-managed but does not interfere with monetary policy. This separation is crucial for central bank independence.

The Bank is accountable to Parliament. The Governor testifies before the House of Commons Standing Committee on Finance twice per year (after each Monetary Policy Report). This transparency enhances credibility.

image.alt: Diagram: Governing Council (6 members) sets policy; Board of Directors (14 members) oversees management; Minister of Finance appoints directors.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.4: Bank of Canada governance structure. The Governing Council sets monetary policy; the Board of Directors oversees management.

examples.explanation: The Governing Council meets 8 times per year. Staff present economic projections (Monetary Policy Report). The Council discusses the outlook and decides on the overnight rate target. The decision is announced at 10:00 AM ET. The Governor holds a press conference to explain the decision.

Tiff Macklem was appointed Governor in 2020 for a 7-year term. The Board of Directors (with Cabinet approval) made the appointment. Macklem had previously been Senior Deputy Governor (2010-2014). The appointment process is non-political, enhancing independence.

classifications.value: Before the pandemic, the Bank used a corridor system with scarce reserves and active open market operations
characteristics: Overnight rate target ± 25 basis points (e.g., target 5.00%, band 4.75% to 5.25%).
Deposit rate (floor): interest paid on settlement balances (bottom of band).
Bank Rate (ceiling): interest charged on overnight loans (top of band).
Active open market operations: SPRAs (add reserves) and LLAs (drain reserves).
Scarce reserves: reserves just sufficient to meet demand (\$10-20 billion).
Daily fine-tuning to keep overnight rate near target.
classifications.behavior:
Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the Bank of Canada operated a traditional corridor system. The Bank kept reserves scarce (just enough to meet demand) and used daily open market operations to fine-tune liquidity. SPRAs (Sale and Repurchase Agreements) added reserves when the overnight rate was above target; LLAs (Liquidity Adjustment Auctions) drained reserves when the rate was below target. The corridor (deposit rate floor, Bank Rate ceiling) bounded the overnight rate.

This system worked well for decades but required active management. The Bank's trading desk had to forecast daily reserve demand and conduct operations accordingly. Reserves were typically \$10-20 billion.

The corridor system was designed for a world of scarce reserves. When the Bank engaged in QE during the pandemic, reserves became ample, and the corridor system became unworkable. The Bank shifted to a floor system in April 2020.

image.alt: Graph: Historical corridor system with scarce reserves, active OMOs, overnight rate within band.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.5: The Bank of Canada's historical corridor system (pre-COVID). Scarce reserves required active open market operations to keep the overnight rate within the 50bp band.

examples.explanation: If the overnight rate was above target, the Bank would conduct a SPRA (Sale and Repurchase Agreement), buying government bonds from primary dealers and injecting reserves. This added liquidity, pushing the overnight rate down toward target.
Before COVID, settlement balances (reserves) were typically \$10-20 billion. The Bank kept reserves scarce to make its open market operations effective.

classifications.value: Since April 2020, the Bank has operated a floor system with ample reserves, using the deposit rate as the primary tool
characteristics: Deposit rate (interest on settlement balances) acts as a floor for the overnight rate.
Banks will not lend reserves below the deposit rate (can earn risk-free return).
Overnight rate target is set at the deposit rate (or slightly above).
Ample reserves from QE (settlement balances > \$300 billion).
Bank Rate set 25bp above deposit rate (ceiling, backstop).
Passive open market operations (no daily fine-tuning needed).
Shift occurred in April 2020 due to QE-induced ample reserves.
classifications.behavior:
Since April 2020, the Bank of Canada has operated a floor system. The deposit rate (interest on settlement balances) acts as a floor: banks will not lend reserves below this rate because they can earn the deposit rate risk-free by leaving them at the Bank. The overnight rate target is set at the deposit rate (or slightly above). The Bank Rate (ceiling) is set 25 basis points above the deposit rate as a backstop.

The shift was necessitated by the Bank's QE program. From March to April 2020, the Bank purchased large quantities of government bonds, injecting massive reserves into the banking system. Settlement balances grew from \$10-20 billion to over \$300 billion. With reserves now ample, the corridor system (which required scarce reserves) became unworkable. The Bank announced the shift to a floor system in April 2020.

Under the floor system, the Bank does not need to conduct daily open market operations. The deposit rate does the work. The overnight rate stays near the deposit rate without active intervention. The Bank may still conduct occasional operations (e.g., to manage year-end liquidity), but fine-tuning is no longer required.

This framework aligns Canada with the Federal Reserve, which has used a floor system since 2008. It is simpler and more reliable than the corridor system.

image.alt: Graph: Current floor system with ample reserves, deposit rate floor, overnight rate trading near floor, passive OMOs.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.6: The Bank of Canada's current floor system (post-COVID). The deposit rate acts as a floor; ample reserves keep the overnight rate near the floor without active open market operations.

examples.explanation: In July 2023, the Bank raised the overnight rate target to 5.00%. The deposit rate was set to 5.00% (floor) and the Bank Rate to 5.25% (ceiling). With ample reserves (over \$300 billion), the overnight rate immediately moved to 5.00% without any open market operations. The floor system worked smoothly.

As of 2024, settlement balances (reserves) are about \$200-300 billion, down from the peak of \$400 billion but still ample. The Bank is conducting quantitative tightening (QT) to reduce reserves, but reserves remain ample, so the floor system continues to operate.

principles.principle: The Bank of Canada Shifted from a Corridor System to a Floor System Due to QE-Induced Ample Reserves
principles.statement: Before COVID-19, the Bank of Canada operated a corridor system with scarce reserves, requiring daily open market operations. When the Bank launched QE in March-April 2020, reserves became ample (from \$10-20 billion to over \$300 billion). The corridor system (which relies on scarce reserves) became unworkable. The Bank announced a shift to a floor system in April 2020. Under the floor system, the deposit rate (interest on settlement balances) acts as a floor, and the overnight rate stays near the floor without active operations. This change aligns Canada with the Federal Reserve's framework. The shift demonstrates how central bank operating frameworks must adapt to changes in reserve levels. If reserves eventually return to scarce levels (through QT), the Bank could theoretically return to a corridor system, but most observers expect the floor system to persist.
principles.application: The Bank of Canada currently uses a floor system. Analysts should monitor the deposit rate as the key policy rate, not the overnight rate target (which is now equal to the deposit rate). The Bank Rate (ceiling) is less important as a backstop. The Bank may conduct occasional open market operations (e.g., for year-end liquidity), but daily fine-tuning is a thing of the past. For Canadians, the floor system is simpler and more transparent.
principles.example: In 2023, the Bank raised the deposit rate from 4.75% to 5.00%. The overnight rate immediately moved to 5.00% without any open market operations. This would not have been possible under the corridor system, which would have required active SPRAs or LLAs to keep the rate near target.

image.alt: Graph: Overnight rate vs. deposit rate (2020-2024); perfect tracking after April 2020, showing floor system effectiveness.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.7: Since April 2020, the overnight rate has tracked the deposit rate perfectly, demonstrating the effectiveness of the floor system.

principles.principle: The Bank of Canada's Inflation Targeting Framework Has Been Highly Successful and Provides a Model for Other Central Banks
principles.statement: Canada's inflation-targeting framework (2% target since 1991) has been one of the most successful monetary policy frameworks in history. Inflation has averaged 2% for over three decades, and the economy has been more stable than before. The framework has anchored expectations, giving households and businesses confidence that inflation will remain low. The Bank's transparency (Monetary Policy Reports, press conferences, testimony) has enhanced credibility. The framework has been renewed every 5 years, with the 2021 renewal reaffirming the 2% target. Many other central banks (including the Fed in 2012) have adopted inflation targeting, following Canada's lead. The success of the framework is a testament to the Bank's independence, credibility, and skillful management.
principles.application: The Bank of Canada should maintain its inflation-targeting framework. The target should remain 2% (with a 1-3% range). The Bank should continue to publish Monetary Policy Reports and fan charts. The Bank should also consider the employment mandate (added in 2021) but keep inflation as the primary objective. For Canadians, the framework provides a stable environment for saving, borrowing, and investment.
principles.example: During the 2008 crisis, inflation fell below 1%. The Bank cut rates to 0.25% and provided forward guidance. Inflation returned to 2% by 2010. During the 2021-2022 inflation surge, the Bank raised rates aggressively. Inflation fell to 3% by 2024. The framework allowed the Bank to respond flexibly while maintaining credibility.

image.alt: Graph: Canadian inflation (1990-2024) vs. US inflation; both have been stable, but Canada's has been closer to target.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.8: Canadian inflation has been remarkably stable since the adoption of inflation targeting in 1991, averaging near 2% and providing a model for other central banks.

worked_examples.title: Understanding the Floor System: Deposit Rate as Policy Rate

image.alt: Table: Deposit rate 5.00%, overnight rate 5.00%, Bank Rate 5.25%; ample reserves $300B.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.9: Worked example: understanding the Bank of Canada's current floor system.

worked_examples.problem:
The Bank of Canada currently operates a floor system with the following rates:

• Deposit rate (interest on settlement balances): 5.00%
• Overnight rate target: 5.00% (equal to deposit rate)
• Bank Rate: 5.25%
• Settlement balances (reserves): \$300 billion (ample)

a) Why do banks not lend reserves below 5.00%?
b) If the overnight rate rises to 5.10%, what should happen?
c) If the overnight rate falls to 4.95%, what should happen?
d) How does the Bank keep the overnight rate at 5.00% without active open market operations?
e) If the Bank wanted to tighten policy by 25 basis points, what would it do?

step_by_step.explanation: Banks can earn 5.00% risk-free by holding settlement balances at the Bank of Canada (deposit rate). Lending reserves to another bank at a rate below 5.00% would be irrational because the lending bank would earn less than the risk-free rate. Therefore, the deposit rate acts as a floor for the overnight rate.

If the overnight rate rises to 5.10%, banks have an arbitrage opportunity: borrow reserves at the Bank Rate (5.25%? Actually 5.10% is above the deposit rate but below the Bank Rate). In a floor system, the overnight rate should not deviate significantly from the deposit rate. If it does, banks could lend reserves at 5.10% (earning 10bp above the deposit rate). This would attract reserves into the overnight market, pushing the rate back down toward 5.00%. In practice, with ample reserves, the overnight rate stays very close to the deposit rate.

If the overnight rate falls to 4.95% (below the 5.00% deposit rate), banks have an arbitrage opportunity: borrow in the overnight market at 4.95% and deposit the funds at the Bank at 5.00%, earning 5 basis points risk-free. This arbitrage will push the overnight rate back up to 5.00%.

step_by_step.step: How the Bank keeps the rate at 5.00% without active OMOs
step_by_step.explanation: The Bank does not need active open market operations because the deposit rate floor does the work. With ample reserves (\$300 billion), banks have no incentive to lend below the deposit rate. The overnight rate naturally stays at the deposit rate. The Bank simply sets the deposit rate, and the market does the rest. This is the essence of the floor system.

To tighten policy by 25 basis points, the Bank would raise the deposit rate by 25 basis points (from 5.00% to 5.25%). The overnight rate target would also rise to 5.25%. The Bank Rate would rise to 5.50% (still 25bp above the deposit rate). The Bank would not need to conduct open market operations; the overnight rate would immediately move to the new deposit rate.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Deposit rate is a risk-free floor; lending below it is irrational. b) Arbitrage (lending at 5.10%) would push rate down. c) Arbitrage (borrowing at 4.95%, depositing at 5.00%) would push rate up. d) Deposit rate floor does the work; ample reserves make it self-enforcing. e) Raise deposit rate by 25bp to 5.25%.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the floor system mechanics to understand how the Bank of Canada implements monetary policy post-COVID.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Historical Corridor vs. Current Floor System

image.alt: Table: Pre-COVID (corridor, scarce reserves, active OMOs) vs. Post-COVID (floor, ample reserves, passive OMOs).
image.caption: Figure 6.5.10: Worked example: comparing the Bank of Canada's historical corridor system with its current floor system.

worked_examples.problem:
Compare the Bank of Canada's historical corridor system (pre-COVID) with its current floor system (post-COVID).

a) How did reserve levels differ between the two systems?
b) How did the Bank keep the overnight rate near target in each system?
c) Why did the Bank shift from corridor to floor?
d) Which system requires more active open market operations?
e) If the Bank reduces reserves back to pre-COVID levels through QT, could it return to a corridor system?

step_by_step.explanation:
Pre-COVID (corridor): Scarce reserves (\$10-20 billion). The Bank kept reserves just sufficient to meet demand.
Post-COVID (floor): Ample reserves (\$200-300 billion). QE injected massive reserves.

Pre-COVID (corridor): Active open market operations (SPRAs to add reserves, LLAs to drain reserves). Daily fine-tuning.
Post-COVID (floor): Deposit rate floor. Banks will not lend below deposit rate. No active OMOs needed.

The Bank engaged in large-scale QE during the COVID-19 pandemic (March-April 2020), injecting massive reserves into the banking system. Reserves became ample (from \$10-20B to over \$300B). The corridor system (designed for scarce reserves) became unworkable because the Bank could not fine-tune such a large quantity. The Bank shifted to a floor system in April 2020, aligning with the Federal Reserve's framework.

The historical corridor system required more active open market operations (daily fine-tuning). The current floor system requires fewer active OMOs (passive; the deposit rate does the work). The Bank may still conduct occasional operations (e.g., for year-end liquidity), but not daily.

If the Bank reduces reserves back to pre-COVID levels through quantitative tightening (QT), it could theoretically return to a corridor system. However, most observers expect the Bank to maintain the floor system even with lower reserves, as it is simpler and more reliable. The floor system works with any level of reserves as long as the deposit rate is set appropriately. The Bank has not signaled any intention to return to the corridor system.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Corridor: scarce ($10-20B); Floor: ample ($200-300B). b) Corridor: active OMOs; Floor: deposit rate floor. c) QE created ample reserves, making corridor unworkable. d) Corridor required more active OMOs. e) Theoretically yes, but unlikely; floor system works with any reserve level.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comparing the Bank of Canada's historical and current operating frameworks to understand the evolution of monetary policy implementation.

process_flow.process: Governing Council sets deposit rate (overnight rate target)
process_flow.description: The Governing Council meets 8 times per year to review economic projections and set the deposit rate (which is also the overnight rate target).
process_flow.mechanism: MPR forecasts; discussion of inflation risks; consensus decision; announcement at 10:00 AM ET.

image.alt: Diagram: Governing Council meeting → MPR forecasts → policy decision → deposit rate set.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.11: Step 1 - The Governing Council sets the deposit rate (which is also the overnight rate target).

process_flow.process: Bank sets Bank Rate 25bp above deposit rate (ceiling)
process_flow.description: The Bank sets the Bank Rate (ceiling) 25 basis points above the deposit rate as a backstop liquidity facility.

image.caption: Figure 6.5.12: Step 2 - The Bank sets the Bank Rate (ceiling) 25 basis points above the deposit rate.

process_flow.process: Deposit rate floor keeps overnight rate at target (no active OMOs)
process_flow.description: With ample reserves, the deposit rate acts as a floor. Banks will not lend below this rate, so the overnight rate stays at the deposit rate.
process_flow.mechanism: Deposit rate floor; no active open market operations needed.

image.alt: Diagram: Deposit rate floor → banks hold reserves → overnight rate at deposit rate.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.13: Step 3 - The deposit rate floor keeps the overnight rate at the target without active open market operations.

process_flow.process: Overnight rate transmits to other interest rates (prime rate, mortgages, bonds)
process_flow.description: Changes in the overnight rate affect the prime rate (target + 2.2%), mortgage rates, and other lending rates.
process_flow.mechanism: Prime rate = overnight rate + 2.2%; mortgage rates = prime rate + spread.

image.alt: Flowchart: Overnight rate → prime rate → mortgage rates, business loans → consumption, investment → inflation.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.14: Step 4 - The overnight rate transmits to other interest rates and ultimately to inflation and output.

process_flow.process: Bank monitors inflation and adjusts deposit rate as needed
process_flow.description: The Bank monitors CPI inflation and adjusts the deposit rate to keep inflation near the 2% target.
process_flow.mechanism: If inflation > 2% (and persistent) → raise deposit rate; if inflation < 2% → lower deposit rate.

image.alt: Decision tree: Inflation > 2% → raise deposit rate; inflation < 2% → lower deposit rate.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.15: Step 5 - The Bank monitors inflation and adjusts the deposit rate to maintain the 2% target.

image.alt: Master framework: Governing Council sets deposit rate → Bank Rate ceiling → deposit rate floor → overnight rate at target → transmission to economy → inflation monitoring → policy adjustment.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.16: Complete framework for the Bank of Canada's monetary policy implementation (floor system).

summary: The Bank of Canada is Canada's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight. The Bank's governance structure separates monetary policy (Governing Council: Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, 4 Deputy Governors) from administration (Board of Directors). Since the COVID-19 pandemic (April 2020), the Bank has operated a floor system, having shifted from its traditional corridor system. The shift was necessitated by QE, which injected massive reserves (from \$10-20 billion to over \$300 billion), making reserves ample. Under the floor system, the deposit rate (interest on settlement balances) acts as a floor; banks will not lend reserves below this rate. The overnight rate target is set at the deposit rate. The Bank Rate (ceiling) is set 25bp above the deposit rate as a backstop. No active open market operations are needed for daily fine-tuning. This framework aligns Canada with the Federal Reserve. The Bank has a single mandate: price stability (2% inflation target since 1991), making it one of the most successful inflation-targeting central banks. The Bank publishes Monetary Policy Reports quarterly and the Governor testifies before Parliament twice per year. Understanding the Bank of Canada's structure is essential for analyzing Canadian monetary policy, comparing it to other central banks, and evaluating the evolution from corridor to floor system.
